paragraphs.text: Ref. Ares(2025)9017673 - 22/10/2025 CYBER EXPLORE Excellence in Cybersecurity Research, Innovation and Education
Digital Fairness for Older Adults: Integrating Cyber Safety and Cybersecurity into Age-Specific Consumer Protection

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Date: 21 October 2025

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: This submission argues that digital fairness must explicitly address the cyber safety and cybersecurity vulnerabilities of older adults as a matter of fundamental rights and consumer protection. Drawing on the Charter of Fundamental Rights ('the CFEU') the Treaty on the Functioning of the EU ('TFEU'), EU secondary law provisions on vulnerable consumers including the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive ('UCPD'), the Digital Services Act ('DSA'), the European Accessibility Act ('EAA'), the AI Act, the General Data Protection Regulation ('GDPR'), and the European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles for the Digital Decade, we demonstrate that older adults constitute a particularly vulnerable group requiring targeted cybersecurity protections. The Digital Fairness Act presents a critical opportunity to give effect to an EU age-inclusive digital policy for consumer cybersecurity and to ensure that the EU's Decade of digital transformation respects the dignity, independence, and safety of older Europeans.

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: We make this submission for and on behalf of CyberExplore at Munster Technological University (MTU), Cork, Ireland. The mission of CyberExplore is to strengthen the security of Ireland's digital assets and the resilience of its critical infrastructure. Its core aim is to securely blend the digital world with the physical world, protecting everyone and making systems more resilient to cyber security by fostering skills, research, and innovation. This submission is made on behalf of CyberExplore by Dr Hazel Murray, Chair of Cybersecurity, MTU, Dr Deirdre Leahy, Researcher and Lecturer in Cybersecurity Law and GRC.

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: We welcome the European Commission's proposal for a Digital Fairness Act and strongly support its core objectives: enhancing enforcement of consumer protection legislation, safeguarding vulnerable consumers, and promoting fairness in digital markets. These priorities are particularly relevant to the cybersecurity domain, where consumers face increasing exposure to online threats and are at greater risk of experiencing relational vulnerabilities. Strengthened legal safeguards intermediated through consumer protection law and clearer enforcement mechanisms can play a

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: ^{}[] CYBER EXPLORE Excellence in Cybersecurity Research, Innovation and Education

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: vital role in mitigating cybersecurity-related risks and ensuring equitable treatment of all users in digital environments.

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: Stronger legal protections can also be channelled to mitigate cybersecurity-related risks while promoting equitable treatment for all users, regardless of age, ability, or digital proficiency. While this is an issue for all age cohorts, it is especially critical for older adult populations. Older users and the elderly often encounter disproportionate risks in digital environments due to lower digital literacy, limited access to technical support, and heightened susceptibility to manipulation or fraud. Addressing these challenges is not only a matter of social justice and equality, but also of consumer protection. Ensuring that consumer protection safeguards are responsive to meeting the cybersecurity needs of all cohorts, but especially ageing populations, is essential for building a fair and resilient digital society.

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: In this submission, 'cybersecurity' refers to the measures, practices, and obligations that protect consumers, particularly older adults, from cyber-enabled harm during their engagement with digital interfaces, online platforms and digital services. It encompasses both technical protections and the design, accessibility, and usability of those protections.

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: The key components of this concept of cybersecurity are the suite of technical measures (e.g. encryption, secure authentication, vulnerability, malware and phishing detection), design and accessibility features (e.g. clear, accessible security warnings and notifications, default security settings), platform-level features (e.g. monitoring, take-down and prevention of cyber-enabled scams, age-aware platform design), and educational components (e.g. communication about risks, accessible supports for incident response) which can be incorporated into platform and user interface design.

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

evidence_quotes.quote: Providers of digital interfaces, online platforms and digital services should bear express obligations to design and implement cyber-secure systems.

paragraphs.text: To be clear, this conceptualisation does not structure cybersecurity as the exclusive responsibility of the consumer. Providers of digital interfaces, online platforms and digital services should bear express obligations to design and implement cyber-secure systems. Moreover, this concept of cybersecurity is not achieved through technical measures alone but requires accessibility, usability, and design considerations that make security practical for diverse user populations. Cybersecurity protections should be adequate to these objectives and enable older adults to participate safely and independently in digital environments and not restrict their access or decision-making autonomy. This framing integrates cybersecurity into existing EU consumer protection principles rather than treating it as a separate technical, security or IT issue.

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

section_path: SUBMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION CONSULTATION ON THE DIGITAL FAIRNESS ACT

paragraphs.text: The 2020 EU Commission Survey on "Scams and Fraud Experienced by Consumers" (the '2020 Survey') confirms the role of the internet as a vehicle for online crime directly targeted at the financial and transactional security of consumers. The 2020 Survey found that the “...probability of experiencing at least one of the types of fraud goes up by 25 percentage points for frequent internet users (at least once a week) compared to someone who hardly ever or never uses the internet.” Frequency of internet use was also identified as a contributing factor to exposure, “... with knock-on effects for confident and secure internet use (at p.44). These risks also present a chilling effect for cybersecure internet use and pose a challenge for the cybersecurity of online transactions, especially by older users. As the 2020 Survey noted, education and awareness campaigns may not reach all internet user profiles, “... especially not those who can least afford to be the victim of a scam or fraud in terms of their socio-economic situation.”

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
personalisation practices

paragraphs.text: Protection against marketplace and economic detriment lies at the core of consumer law. The Commission's Fitness Check of EU Consumer Law on Digital Fairness ('the Fitness Check') has demonstrated the limitations of the existing consumer law framework, relative to a range of online architectures such as dark patterns, addictive designs, and personalisation practices across as range from targeted advertising and personal data analysis to unfair and exploitative monetisation practices. These deceptive architectures not only exploit the financial and economic well-being of consumers, but they also expose consumers to personal detriment. Although the Fitness Check gave some consideration to cybersecurity as a consumer protection issue, it did not do so extensively as a standalone topic.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading online advertising

paragraphs.text: Through the Consumer Protection Co-Operation ('CPC') network, the EU has already demonstrated that co-ordinated and effective cross-border action can successfully intervene to diminish the consumer impacts of illegal online practices. For example, during the Covid 19 crisis the CPC network requested online platforms to better identify, remove and prevent the reappearance of millions of illegal practices around increased reports of misleading online advertising, scams and fraud (Staff Working Document on Fitness Check of EU consumer law on digital fairness, p.33). Utilising existing law, this successful collective enforcement action was taken in response to cross-border illegal activity. A more consistent approach to cybersecurity of consumers would, however, demand a specific obligation on providers of digital interfaces, online platforms and digital services to maintain a cybersecurity posture that prioritises a secure transactional environment for consumers, especially older adults. This is justified because, as a population sector, older adults are disproportionately vulnerable when targeted by online crime, and thereby experience greater adverse social, economic and personal impacts.
^{}[] CYBER EXPLORE Excellence in Cybersecurity Research, Innovation and Education

evidence_quotes.quote: Certain types of cyber exploitation experienced by this population cohort, which manifests in cyber-enabled crimes such as scams, fraud and identity theft, can be attributable to age-related vulnerabilities (Lazarus, 2025).

paragraphs.text: The European Union faces unprecedented demographic aging. By 2050, over 30% of the EU population will be aged 65 or older. This demographic shift coincides with the accelerating digitalisation of essential services—banking, healthcare, public administration, commerce, and social participation. (European Commission, 2024), (European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights., 2023). As a sector of the population, older adults face unique cybersecurity vulnerabilities attributable to a diverse range of causations, including but not limited to restricted digital literacy, cognitive vulnerabilities, and economic resources (Havers et al, 2024), (Kemp & Erades Pérez, 2023), (Morrison, Coventry, & Briggs P., 2020), (Pacheco, 2024). Certain types of cyber exploitation experienced by this population cohort, which manifests in cyber-enabled crimes such as scams, fraud and identity theft, can be attributable to age-related vulnerabilities (Lazarus, 2025).

evidence_quotes.quote: due to cybersecurity concerns, 65% of older adults interviewed (n=275) had discontinued using an internet-based service in the previous year

Exclusion from digital interactions due to age-related cybersecurity concerns exposes older population cohorts to potentially greater risk of adverse economic, social and welfare outcomes (Morrison et al., 2023), (Gruben, Sheil et al., 2025).

paragraphs.text: A recent New Zealand study among older adults identified that due to cybersecurity concerns, 65% of older adults interviewed (n=275) had discontinued using an internet-based service in the previous year, reflecting a common mitigation strategy identified in prior qualitative studies (Pacheco, 2024), (Feng, 2023, July), (Frik, 2019). Given the horizontal breadth of the countries studied, this may be a universally occurring problem, and if so, it would also be observed in the EU if a similar study were conducted here. Exclusion from digital interactions due to age-related cybersecurity concerns exposes older population cohorts to potentially greater risk of adverse economic, social and welfare outcomes (Morrison et al., 2023), (Gruben, Sheil et al., 2025).

evidence_quotes.quote: Accessible cybersecurity is an issue for all population cohorts, and accessible cybersecurity should be designed and implemented into all digital systems ‘...or risk excluding and marginalising swathes of the population who cannot use these systems in the same way as abled users’ (Renaud & Coles-Kemp, 2022).

Age-related cybersecurity vulnerability is a cluster of distinct, overlapping vulnerabilities that interact to create heightened cybersecurity risk for older adults’ digital activity and online participation.
It spans a range of age-related and situational factors that present themselves in the digital environment in a manner that may impede or diminish the transactional capacity of the older adult.

paragraphs.text: Accessible cybersecurity is an issue for all population cohorts, and accessible cybersecurity should be designed and implemented into all digital systems ‘...or risk excluding and marginalising swathes of the population who cannot use these systems in the same way as abled users’ (Renaud & Coles-Kemp, 2022). Age-related cybersecurity vulnerability is a cluster of distinct, overlapping vulnerabilities that interact to create heightened cybersecurity risk for older adults’ digital activity and online participation. It spans a range of age-related and situational factors that present themselves in the digital environment in a manner that may impede or diminish the transactional capacity of the older adult.
A non-exhaustive list of these vulnerabilities comprises:
^{}[] CYBER EXPLORE Excellence in Cybersecurity Research, Innovation and Education

evidence_quotes.quote: improved design, accessible security interfaces

paragraphs.text: Cognitive and digital literacy vulnerabilities: Older adults often possess lower baseline digital literacy resulting from having conducted most of their financial, social, and administrative transactions in pre-internet environments. This manifests in limited threat recognition capacity, technical comprehension gaps and authentication/password management challenges. These vulnerabilities are not inevitable but are attributable to cohort effects and differential exposure to digital environments. They are amenable to intervention through improved design, accessible security interfaces, and targeted education calibrated to older learners' needs.

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive trust-based requests

paragraphs.text: Behavioural and trust-related vulnerabilities: Older adults' behavioural patterns create distinct cybersecurity vulnerabilities, that are exploitable through social engineering and psychological manipulation. Older adults are susceptible to deceptive trust-based requests (e.g. deceptive communications purporting to come from banking, social services etc), reduced awareness of evolving scam tactics, coupled with social isolation and information asymmetries attributable to unfamiliarity with digital scamming techniques.

evidence_quotes.quote: Accessibility barriers create a specific form of exclusion: security features that cannot be accessed due to functional limitations effectively prevent older users from secure participation in digital services, forcing them to either abandon the service or use insecure workarounds.

paragraphs.text: Physical and accessibility-based vulnerabilities: Many older adults have functional limitations that intersect with cybersecurity to create specific barriers such as vision-related, hearing and motor-related limitations, coupled with cognitive decline associated with aging. Accessibility barriers create a specific form of exclusion: security features that cannot be accessed due to functional limitations effectively prevent older users from secure participation in digital services, forcing them to either abandon the service or use insecure workarounds.

evidence_quotes.quote: Older adults may lack access to or knowledge of online identity theft protection, or the technical capacity to remediate compromised accounts.
increasing the urgency of prevention-focused obligations on service providers.

paragraphs.text: Economic and social vulnerabilities: The consequences of cybersecurity incidents fall more heavily on older adults due to economic and social factors, with reduced capacity to seek or implement remediation. Older adults on fixed incomes or with limited savings experience disproportionate economic harm from online fraud or identity theft. Older adults may lack access to or knowledge of online identity theft protection, or the technical capacity to remediate compromised accounts. These economic and social factors do not create the initial cybersecurity vulnerability, but they intensify and amplify the harm and impede recovery, increasing the urgency of prevention-focused obligations on service providers.

Digital exclusion and withdrawal: Digital exclusion and withdrawal is a direct response to cybersecurity concerns such as those identified in the New Zealand study discussed above (Pacheco, 2024). It results in social exclusion and has discriminatory impacts for older adults. This category of vulnerability is distinct
^{}[] CYBER EXPLORE Excellence in Cybersecurity Research, Innovation and Education

evidence_quotes.quote: It represents a failure of service design to accommodate older users' legitimate security concerns.

paragraphs.text: from the others in that it is a consequence of inadequate platform-level cybersecurity rather than a characteristic of older adults themselves. It represents a failure of service design to accommodate older users' legitimate security concerns.

Substantial academic work exists on consumer cybersecurity and older adults' digital and online vulnerabilities, but this knowledge may not be fully reflected in binding legal obligations that protect the cybersecurity needs of this population cohort. The gaps identified by research also reflect a gap between existing law and proposed reforms, especially those promised by the European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles for the Digital Decade.

The European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles for the Digital Decade (2023) ("the Declaration") provides a critical policy foundation for the proposals in this submission. The Declaration underscores that digital transformation must uphold equality and inclusion, ensuring that "no one is left behind" (European Union, 2023). It explicitly commits to procuring access to technology that is designed for all, including older persons (Article 2). It also commits to fostering a safe and secure digital environment grounded in the protection of fundamental rights, while promoting a technical environment that empowers consumers to make autonomous, informed choices, and clearly defines responsibilities for platforms, especially large players and gatekeepers, within the Digital Single Market (Article 11).

section_path: 1.3 European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles for the Digital Decade

paragraphs.text: In Article 16 the Declaration recognises the right to a protected, safe, and secure digital environment and access to "...digital technologies, products and services that are by design safe, secure, and privacy-protective, resulting in a high level of confidentiality, integrity, availability and authenticity of the information processed." These are the essential criteria for a cybersafe online environment and the cornerstone of cybersecurity in online platforms and digital services.

section_path: 1.3 European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles for the Digital Decade

evidence_quotes.quote: data breaches
identity theft or manipulation

paragraphs.text: The Declaration commits to fairness and inclusion of "...elderly people, people living in rural areas, persons with disabilities, or marginalised, vulnerable or disenfranchised people and those who act on their behalf" in digital transformation (European Union, 2023, p. Article 2.b). It expressly acknowledges the interactions of cybersecurity and human empowerment in digital spaces and commits to "...protecting the interests of people, businesses and public institutions against cybersecurity risks and cybercrime including data breaches and identity theft or manipulation" (European Union, 2023, p. Article 16.a). Cybersecurity vulnerabilities that disproportionately deter older adults from engaging online therefore risk infringing the fundamental rights guaranteed by

section_path: 1.3 European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles for the Digital Decade

paragraphs.text: We acknowledge that the Declaration is not legally binding but represents the shared political commitment of the European Parliament, Council, and Commission. The Digital Fairness Act provides the legislative opportunity to address the legal gap in cybersecurity protections for older adults. Protecting the interests of older adults against cybersecurity risks and cybercrime, including data breaches and identity theft or manipulation, requires robust mandatory cybersecurity requirements where digital fairness is the cornerstone principle for ensuring transparency and security by design.

section_path: 1.3 European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles for the Digital Decade

evidence_quotes.quote: cybersecurity measures calibrated to the vulnerabilities of older adults
address the cybersecurity and digital safety needs of older adults.

paragraphs.text: While consumer cybersecurity is well-researched academically, there remains a significant gap in EU consumer law, since existing legislation does not explicitly require service providers to implement cybersecurity measures calibrated to the vulnerabilities of older adults. Current EU consumer protection recognises vulnerable consumers but lacks specific mechanisms to address the cybersecurity and digital safety needs of older adults. The Digital Fairness Act is an opportunity to close this gap in the legal framework. It can do so by:
Operationalising primary EU law
Synchronising the provisions of secondary EU law

evidence_quotes.quote: cybersecurity vulnerability of elderly users.

paragraphs.text: Implementing the European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles commitment to protecting the cybersecurity vulnerability of elderly users.
Creating age-appropriate cybersecurity standards under consumer protection law.
MTU Oilscoil Teicneolaiochta na Mumhan Munster Technological University ^{}[] CYBER EXPLORE Excellence in Cybersecurity Research, Innovation and Education
Article 25 states: "The Union recognises and respects the rights of the elderly to lead a life of dignity and independence and to participate in social and cultural life."

section_path: 2.1 Article 25 CFEU: Rights of the elderly

paragraphs.text: Not all cyber-enabled crimes are financially motivated. However, cyber-enabled crimes with financial consequences for victims, such as scams, fraud, and identity theft, constitute a profound violation of human dignity, particularly when older adults are systematically targeted through digital services that digital providers know, or ought to know, lack adequate safeguards against age-specific risks (Lazarus, 2025). Such deficiencies also erode digital independence, as the ability to engage safely with online services is a prerequisite for autonomy (Gruben, Sheil et al., 2025). When cybersecurity failures disproportionately affect older adults, they foster dependency, compel reliance on intermediaries for routine transactions, and exclude this demographic from autonomous digital engagement. Moreover, as social and cultural participation increasingly occurs in digital spaces, vulnerabilities that prompt older adults to withdraw, whether due to fear, victimisation, or diminished confidence, undermine the participatory dimension of Article 25, effectively curtailing their right to full societal inclusion.

section_path: 2.1 Article 25 CFEU: Rights of the elderly

paragraphs.text: The European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights (FRA) report "Fundamental rights of older people: ensuring access to public services in digital societies" (European Union Agency for Fundamental Rights., 2023) explicitly recognises that digital exclusion threatens fundamental rights, including the right to dignity and independence under Article 25. We argue that refraining from internet use due to concerns about cybersecurity risks and cybercrime constitutes a form of social exclusion and has discriminatory impacts on older persons, contrary to Article 25 and Article 21 CFEU (see below).

section_path: 2.1 Article 25 CFEU: Rights of the elderly

evidence_quotes.quote: risk of digital exclusion
barriers older people may face in the digital environment.

paragraphs.text: Published in September 2023, the report responds to the Council of the EU's call to explore the impact of digitalisation on fundamental rights, active participation, and wellbeing of older persons. The research covered all EU Member States and analysed legislation, policies, and practices concerning digital inclusion of older adults. It found that national legislation across EU countries does not always acknowledge the risk of digital exclusion and does not address the barriers older people may face in the digital environment.

section_path: 2.1 Article 25 CFEU: Rights of the elderly

paragraphs.text: This gap is critical for understanding the cybersecurity needs of older adults. If legislation fails to acknowledge digital exclusion risks, it necessarily fails to address cybersecurity vulnerabilities that compound and exploit those exclusion risks. The FRA Report identifies three levels of digital divide affecting older persons, the second and

section_path: 2.1 Article 25 CFEU: Rights of the elderly

section_path: 2.1 Article 25 CFEU: Rights of the elderly

evidence_quotes.quote: barriers to digital access attributable to inadequacies in the cybersecurity framework.
Inadequate cybersecurity measures demand additional digital skills that are not easily acquired by older adults
language barriers in security communications.
fear of cybercrime,
victimisation leading to digital withdrawal,
lack of trust in digital services due to security concerns
inability to participate in digital economy and society due to security barriers.

paragraphs.text: third of which concern barriers to digital access attributable to inadequacies in the cybersecurity framework. Second level digital divides comprise inadequate skills and digital literacy resulting in poor understanding of basic cybersecurity protections (e.g. password hygiene, phishing recognition), inability to navigate complex security settings, inability to recognise and respond to cyber threats, and language barriers in security communications. Third level digital divides are to be found in barriers to digital participation such as fear of cybercrime, victimisation leading to digital withdrawal, lack of trust in digital services due to security concerns and inability to participate in digital economy and society due to security barriers. Failures of cybersecurity compound all three divides. Inadequate cybersecurity measures demand additional digital skills that are not easily acquired by older adults, and it prevents meaningful digital participation due to fear and actual harm.

section_path: 2.1 Article 25 CFEU: Rights of the elderly

evidence_quotes.quote: cybersecurity vulnerabilities associated with age.

paragraphs.text: The FRA framework demonstrates that cybersecurity is not separate from digital inclusion but central to it. Older adults cannot be digitally included if they are systematically undermined by inadequate platform and provider obligations to provide cybersecure access to the digital environment. We argue that current legislation lacks age-specific digital rights protection, and while most Member States have general equality legislation, few specifically address digital exclusion of older persons or cybersecurity vulnerabilities associated with age. Furthermore, even where policies exist supporting digital inclusion, implementation mechanisms are weak, particularly regarding cybersecurity education and scam prevention for older users.

section_path: 2.1 Article 25 CFEU: Rights of the elderly

evidence_quotes.quote: cybersecurity measures designed exclusively for digitally native users, creating disproportionate vulnerability for older users

paragraphs.text: Article 21(1) CFEU prohibits discrimination based on age. When digital services implement cybersecurity measures designed exclusively for digitally native users, creating disproportionate vulnerability for older users, this raises age discrimination concerns.

Since Mangold v Helm (C-144/04) and Kücükdeveci v Swedex (C-555/07) the principle of non-discrimination on grounds of age is a general principle of EU law, applicable horizontally in employment contexts. By extension, consumer protection contexts involving systemic age-based disadvantage that is attributable to platform and provider deficiencies in cybersecurity protection warrant similar scrutiny.

In addition, Article 26 CFEU which recognises the full social, community and occupational integration of persons with disabilities, extends to supporting the full participation of older persons. Many older persons have disabilities (vision, hearing, dexterity, cognitive) that intersect with cybersecurity barriers. It is our position that inadequate and/or in accessible security features exclude this population.
^{}[] CYBER EXPLORE Excellence in Cybersecurity Research, Innovation and Education
Article 169(1) TFEU mandates that the Union "shall contribute to protecting the health, safety and economic interests of consumers" and promote their "right to information" and "education."
It is our submission that for older consumers:
Economic interests are threatened by financial scams exploiting cybersecurity gaps.
Safety includes protection from cyber exploitation and psychological harm.

A consumer right to information is the foundational paradigm of consumer protection. The provision of information that addresses informational imbalances, includes accessible, age-appropriate cybersecurity education.
Education must address the specific cyber literacy needs of older learners.

Article 169(2)(a) authorises harmonisation measures "which support, supplement and monitor the policy pursued by the Member States." We submit that recognition of the special needs of older adults through adoption of a concept of digital fairness that addresses those needs falls squarely within the competence of the Digital Fairness Act.

evidence_quotes.quote: By recognising the vulnerability of consumers in their economic transaction decisions, Article 5(3) has laid the groundwork for extension of the vulnerability concept to areas that might not have been relevant to the pre-digital age.

paragraphs.text: Although criticised for its limited adaptability to the digital age, the UCPD is foundational for addressing cyber exploitation of older adults. By recognising the vulnerability of consumers in their economic transaction decisions, Article 5(3) has laid the groundwork for extension of the vulnerability concept to areas that might not have been relevant to the pre-digital age. Even within these limitations, Article 5(3) UCPD;

3.1 Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (2005/29/EC) ('UCPD'): The vulnerable consumer provisions

evidence_quotes.quote: materially distort the economic behaviour

paragraphs.text: The decisions of the CJEU support this interpretation. In Orange România (C-61/19) it confirmed that information duties must enable consumers to make informed decisions, having already emphasised in Purely Creative and Others (C-428/11) that the UCPD requires assessment of whether practices "materially distort the economic behaviour" of consumers. As the FRA demonstrates, cybersecurity threats to older adults, such as cyber scams facilitated by inadequate platform security, directly distort older consumers' economic behaviours.

section_path: 3. Secondary EU Law: Protection for Vulnerable Consumers
3.1 Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (2005/29/EC) ('UCPD'): The vulnerable consumer provisions

evidence_quotes.quote: they engage in practices that materially distort the economic behaviour of older consumers in ways traders can reasonably foresee

paragraphs.text: they engage in practices that materially distort the economic behaviour of older consumers in ways traders can reasonably foresee, constituting unfair commercial practices under Article 5(3).

section_path: 3. Secondary EU Law: Protection for Vulnerable Consumers
3.1 Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (2005/29/EC) ('UCPD'): The vulnerable consumer provisions

evidence_quotes.quote: older persons are a "clearly identifiable group" who are "particularly vulnerable" due to age
Inadequate cybersecurity that exploits this vulnerability constitutes an unfair commercial practice.

paragraphs.text: However, the obligation to provide age-specific protections can only be inferred from interpretation of Article 5(3) UCPD and it is not given expression via an express and unequivocal obligation to create and maintain cyber-safe systems that provide adequate levels of digital security for older adults. This limits the potential impacts and scope of Article 5(3). The FRA report provides the empirical and rights-based foundation demonstrating that older persons are a "clearly identifiable group" who are "particularly vulnerable" due to age, and that traders "could reasonably be expected to foresee" this vulnerability. Inadequate cybersecurity that exploits this vulnerability constitutes an unfair commercial practice. Cybersecurity measures are foreseeably inadequate where service providers, for example, fail to implement authentication suited to older users reduced digital literacy, neglect to mitigate known scams disproportionately affecting this demographic, communicate security information in inaccessible formats, and impose device or technical requirements that exacerbate the digital divides. This vulnerability and its consequences deserve legislative

section_path: 3. Secondary EU Law: Protection for Vulnerable Consumers
3.1 Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (2005/29/EC) ('UCPD'): The vulnerable consumer provisions

paragraphs.text: Recital 56 DSA explicitly references that "some recipients of the service such as children and the elderly are particularly vulnerable" and gives expression to that recognition by imposing specific obligations on digital platforms. However, to maximise the impact of these obligations, they should also translate into concrete consumer protection-specific cybersecurity obligations.

evidence_quotes.quote: deceive or manipulate recipients or otherwise materially distort or impair the ability of recipients to make free and informed decisions.

paragraphs.text: Article 26 DSA (Design of online interfaces) prohibits platforms from designing interfaces that "deceive or manipulate recipients or otherwise materially distort or impair the ability of recipients to make free and informed decisions."
For older users, this prohibition extends to design features such as:
Security settings buried in complex menu structures
Default security configurations inadequate for vulnerable users
Scam content presented without adequate warnings
Deceptive communications mimicking legitimate security alerts

evidence_quotes.quote: "actual or foreseeable negative effects"
vulnerable groups
By implication, age-based cybersecurity vulnerability must be included in these assessments.

paragraphs.text: Article 34 (Risk assessment) also requires Very Large Online Platforms to assess "actual or foreseeable negative effects" on vulnerable groups. By implication, age-based cybersecurity vulnerability must be included in these assessments.

evidence_quotes.quote: cybersecurity protection for older adults under the DSA.

paragraphs.text: However, to date there are not any specific implementation guidelines that explicitly address cybersecurity protection for older adults under the DSA. The Act's framework provides the legal basis for such arguments, but the scope of an obligation to ensure that older adults can interact with online digital platforms in a cyber safe and secure way remains an area requiring further development through Commission guidelines, national enforcement, or case law. Given the gravity of cybercrimes for the physical and mental well-being of older adults, as well as the potential for adverse social and economic impacts, we submit that the potential for platform-level action through the DSA can be harnessed by recognition of a concept of digital fairness that incorporates the cybersecurity needs of older adults.

The European Accessibility Act (EAA), applicable from 28 June 2025, aims to improve the functioning of the internal market for accessible products and services by removing barriers created by divergent rules in different Member States. It is intended to improve
^{}[] CYBER EXPLORE Excellence in Cybersecurity Research, Innovation and Education

evidence_quotes.quote: access for persons with disabilities and 'persons with functional limitations' such as the elderly.
This concept of adaptation to address functional limitations is foundational for understanding cybersecurity as an accessibility issue for older adults.

paragraphs.text: access for persons with disabilities and 'persons with functional limitations' such as the elderly. It addresses interaction with various barriers that impede access to products and services, leading to a situation that requires those products and services to be adapted to the needs of such persons. This concept of adaptation to address functional limitations is foundational for understanding cybersecurity as an accessibility issue for older adults.

Annex 1- Section 3 EAA establishes accessibility requirements for services. It requires that electronic information, websites, including their related online applications, and mobile device-based services, including mobile applications, must be supplied in a consistent and adequate way, that is "perceivable, operable, understandable and robust."
Cybersecurity is conceptually integral to each dimension of these requirements:
Security warnings and authentication prompts must be perceivable to older users (adequate contrast, font size, clear language)
Security features must be operable by older users with varying dexterity and digital literacy
Security mechanisms must be understandable without advanced technical knowledge
Services must be robust against cyber threats that disproportionately affect older users.

In addition to these general requirements, Annex 1 Section 3 EAA sets specific reliability and security requirements for online consumer banking services, e-commerce services and electronic communications services. The EAA promotes a "universal design" or a "design-for-all" approach, which means that products, environments, programmes and services should be designed to be usable by all people to the greatest extent possible, informed by holistic principles and standards of human-centric cyber security system design (Grobler et al., 2021).

evidence_quotes.quote: inaccessible security features create dual vulnerability.
First, they are exposed to a primary vulnerability caused by inability to use security features (e.g., complex authentication, unreadable security warnings).

paragraphs.text: As evidenced by the FSA, for older adults, inaccessible security features create dual vulnerability. First, they are exposed to a primary vulnerability caused by inability to use security features (e.g., complex authentication, unreadable security warnings). Second, inaccessible security leads to workarounds (writing passwords down, disabling protections) that increase cybersecurity risk.

Security features that fail accessibility standards under the EAA effectively exclude older users from secure digital participation and contribute to digital inequalities and unfairness. This constitutes both an accessibility violation under the EEA and a failure of consumer protection.
^{}[] CYBER EXPLORE Excellence in Cybersecurity Research, Innovation and Education

evidence_quotes.quote: AI systems may affect vulnerable persons, including elderly persons

Article 9(9) requires risk management systems for high-risk AI to consider whether “...in view of its intended purpose the high-risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on persons under the age of 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups.”

paragraphs.text: The AI Act acknowledges that AI systems may affect vulnerable persons, including elderly persons, requiring particular attention to risks. Article 15 mandates cybersecurity for high-risk AI systems. When such systems are used with or marketed to older populations (e.g., health monitoring, financial advice, care coordination), cybersecurity measures must account for age-specific vulnerabilities. Article 9(9) requires risk management systems for high-risk AI to consider whether “...in view of its intended purpose the high-risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on persons under the age of 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups.”

section_path: 3.4 AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689): High-risk systems and vulnerable groups

evidence_quotes.quote: The AI Act protects older adults from exploitation by prohibiting AI systems that take advantage of their vulnerabilities

paragraphs.text: The AI Act protects older adults from exploitation by prohibiting AI systems that take advantage of their vulnerabilities, and it requires cybersecurity measures for high-risk AI systems. However, it does not specifically mandate enhanced cybersecurity protections for older adults as users. Rather, it prohibits AI systems that would exploit their age-related vulnerabilities and requires general cybersecurity standards for AI systems. This creates an obligation against deliberate AI-related harms, but it does not translate into a positive obligation to build age-specific cybersecurity requirements for AI systems. Moreover, the AI Act does not specifically establish special protections for AI systems used with or marketed to older persons. It does not stipulate as to accessibility-compliant cybersecurity as a requirement for AI systems serving older users, scam-detection or elder-fraud-prevention obligations for AI systems.

section_path: 3.4 AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689): High-risk systems and vulnerable groups

paragraphs.text: While the AI Act requires cybersecurity for high-risk systems generally and acknowledges vulnerable persons including the elderly, it does not operationalise these principles into concrete cybersecurity standards calibrated to older users' specific vulnerabilities. This represents a significant regulatory gap that the Digital Fairness Act can address.

section_path: 3.4 AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689): High-risk systems and vulnerable groups

paragraphs.text: The General Data Protection Regulation (EU 2016/679) establishes the legal foundation for a right to information security, and this submission argues that by extension, it should encompass cybersecure online engagements, especially for older persons.

This risk assessment obligation in Article 25 creates a duty to account for vulnerable users, so that:

A risk assessment must consider all factors to select appropriate security measures proportionate to data processing risks.

paragraphs.text: The GDPR framework establishes that security obligations are contextual, and risk adjusted. Controllers cannot apply uniform security standards; they must assess risks specific to their user population.

section_path: 3.5 General Data Protection Regulation ('GDPR'): Establishing a right to cybersecure online interactions for older adults

paragraphs.text: The GDPR establishes a duty of information security proportionate to risk. Article 25 introduces qualified responsibility on data controllers to use technical and organisational measures designed to ensure that personal data processing is compliant with GDPR's provisions and to ensure that consumers' data protection rights are upheld.

section_path: 3.5 General Data Protection Regulation ('GDPR'): Establishing a right to cybersecure online interactions for older adults

evidence_quotes.quote: security-by-design and security-for-vulnerable-users principle
an age-appropriate cybersecure information processing environment

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act should operationalise the GDPR's implicit security-by-design and security-for-vulnerable-users principle by recognising and giving expression to a specific right of older users to an age-appropriate cybersecure information processing environment. The Digital Fairness Act could also streamline interactions between the GDPR and the UCPD by confirming that that GDPR Article 25/32 security failures constitute unfair commercial practices under UCPD Article 5(3) and by requiring accessibility compliance for security features as part of GDPR compliance.

section_path: 3.5 General Data Protection Regulation ('GDPR'): Establishing a right to cybersecure online interactions for older adults

evidence_quotes.quote: with appropriate guardrails around their interactions that address age-specific digital risks.
older adults to engage with digital transactions in a cybersecure and safe way

paragraphs.text: We recognise that this is a novel argument in respect of the nexus between the GDPR and the UCPD. However, if information security is accepted as a dimension of the data protection right, the GDPR only partially protects the cybersecurity vulnerabilities of older adults in the digital processing and collection of their personal data. The Digital Fairness Act presents an opportunity to extend that recognition by confirming that the right to data protection extends to the right of older adults to engage with digital transactions in a cybersecure and safe way, with appropriate guardrails around their interactions that address age-specific digital risks. This recognition would give the right to data protection even greater substantive weight by establishing that equitable, age-appropriate digital security is also a consumer protection right.

section_path: 3.5 General Data Protection Regulation ('GDPR'): Establishing a right to cybersecure online interactions for older adults

paragraphs.text: Examination of these legislative enactments through the lens of cybersecurity is a novel approach to both cybersecurity and consumer protection. We argue for a holistic approach to cybersecurity in the context of consumer online interactions, which goes beyond mere protection of personal data as an isolated asset and recognises the cybersecurity context of digital data in the transactional activities of the consumer, and the underrepresentation of cybersecurity within the concept of consumer protection.
